content.full_text:
WILBUR W. HAMILTON, Executive Director

DIANNE FEINSTEIN, Mayor

Howard M. Wexler, President
Joan-Marie Shelley, Vice President
Charlotte Berk
Dian Blomquist
Rubin Glickman
Melvin D. Lee
Parree Porter

SAN FRANCISCO REDEVELOPMENT AGENCY
939 ELLIS STREET • SAN FRANCISCO 94109
ADDRESS MAIL TO POST OFFICE BOX 645 • SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94101

(415) 771-8800

July 24, 1979

REFER TO:

103-52979-008

Dwight Dickerson, Esq.
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

This is to confirm our telephone conversation of July 20, 1979,
in which you informed me that the test of whether or not a business entity is "doing business" in a jurisdiction (and is, therefore, a "reportable source" of income under the Conflict of
Interest Code) is based upon the nature and the location of the
work done by the entity, not on the location of the entity's
clients. For example, if an Oakland business entity performs
services for a client with offices in both Oakland and San Francisco, and the services relate solely to the client's Oakland
activities, the business entity is not "doing business" in San
Francisco, notwithstanding the fact that the client may otherwise
be doing business in San Francisco. On the other hand, if an
Oakland business entity performs services for a client with offices
in Oakland alone, but the services performed relate to purchase
of property in San Francisco, the business may be deemed to be
"doing business" in San Francisco.

Thank you again for your advice and guidance in this area. I
really appreciate the assistance you have given me in interpreting
various requirements of the Political Reform Act.

Very truly yours,

DAVID J. OSTER
Attorney at Law

40% recycled material

sections.question_synthetic: This document confirms FPPC guidance on whether a business entity is 'doing business' in a jurisdiction for purposes of conflict of interest reporting, specifically addressing whether the location of a client's offices or the nature of work performed determines reportability.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This appears to be incoming correspondence from a private attorney confirming FPPC guidance rather than an official FPPC advice letter response. No formal Q/C sections present. Document references a prior telephone conversation rather than a formal request/response structure. Confidence reduced due to ambiguous classification between correspondence and incoming_request.

Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Mr. Dickerson: This is to confirm our telephone conversation of July 20, 1979, in which you informed me that the test of whether or not a business entity is "doing business" in a jurisdiction (and is, therefore, a "reportable source" of income under the Conflict of Interest Code) is based upon the nature and the location of the work done by the entity, not on the location of the entity's clients. For example, if an Oakland business entity performs services for a client with offices in both Oakland and San Francisco, and the services relate solely to the client's Oakland activities, the business entity is not "doing business" in San Francisco, notwithstanding the fact that the client may otherwise be doing business in San Francisco. On the other hand, if an Oakland business entity performs services for a client with offices in Oakland alone, but the services performed relate to purchase of property in San Francisco, the business may be deemed to be "doing business" in San Francisco. embedding.summary: This is a letter from David J. Oster (Attorney at Law) to FPPC's Dwight Dickerson confirming telephone guidance received on July 20, 1979, regarding the test for determining whether a business entity is 'doing business' in a jurisdiction under the Conflict of Interest Code—based on the nature and location of work performed, not client location.
